facts.text: Marlboro hosts the Gomez Mill House, built 1714 by Sephardic Jewish merchant Luis Moses Gomez, the oldest surviving Jewish dwelling in North America.
The Chapel Hill Bible Church, a mid‑19th‑century Gothic Revival wooden church relocated in 1905, is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.
The Dubois‑Sarles Octagon House, built 1850, is one of New York’s few surviving octagon houses and reflects Orson Squire Fowler’s design ideas.
Marlboro’s early growth was spurred by the arrival of the West Shore Railroad in the 1880s, linking it to broader markets.
The town’s first paper mill, established in the 18th century, marked Marlboro as Ulster County’s pioneer in paper manufacturing.
Marlboro sits on the west bank of the Hudson River at 41°36′17″N 73°58′29″W, giving it a scenic riverside setting.
